questions.question:
A computer has a 256 KByte, 4-way set associative, write back data cache with block size of 32 Bytes. The processor sends 32 bit addresses to the cache controller. Each cache tag directory entry contains, in addition to address tag, 2 valid bits, 1 modified bit and 1 replacement bit. The size of the cache tag directory is:
A. 11 KBits
B. 14 KBits
C. 16 KBits
D. 27 KBits
questions.to_find: Total size of the cache tag directory in KBits

understand.plain: We have a 256 KB 4-way set associative write-back cache with 32-byte blocks. The processor uses 32-bit addresses. Each cache line entry stores an address tag plus a few control bits. We need to find the total storage required for all these tag entries together.

keywords.explain: Cache organized into sets, each holding k lines (ways). A memory block maps to exactly one set but can occupy any of the k lines within that set.
keywords.example: 4-way SA: each set has 4 lines; any block can go to any of those 4 lines.

keywords.explain: The part of cache memory that stores the tags (upper bits of the address) and control bits for each cache line, used to check if a requested block is present.
keywords.example: For a 32-bit address with 5 offset bits and 11 index bits, the tag is 16 bits.

keywords.explain: A cache write policy where data is written to main memory only when the modified cache line is evicted, requiring a 'modified' (dirty) bit to track changes.
keywords.example: Modified bit = 1 means the block in cache differs from the copy in main memory.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='40' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='260' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>32-bit Address Breakdown</text><rect x='10' y='65' width='200' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Tag (16 bits)</text><rect x='210' y='65' width='160' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Index (11 bits)</text><rect x='370' y='65' width='140' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='440' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Offset (5 bits)</text><text x='10' y='140' class='sub ink'>Sets = 256K / (32 x 4) = 2048 sets  |  Lines = 256K/32 = 8192</text><text x='10' y='160' class='sub ink'>Bits/entry = 16 (tag)  |  Total tag bits = 8192 x 16 = 131072 bits = 16 KBits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address split into 16-bit tag, 11-bit set index, and 5-bit block offset. Cache has 2048 sets with 4 ways each = 8192 total lines.

given.aim: Calculate total cache tag directory size in KBits

terms.term: Cache size = 256 KB, block size = 32 B, 4-way SA
terms.example: Each set holds 4 lines; 2048 sets × 4 lines = 8192 total lines
terms.connects: Determines number of tag entries and the index field width

terms.meaning: Full physical address is 32 bits wide, split into tag, index, and offset fields
terms.example: Offset = log2(32) = 5 bits; Index = log2(2048) = 11 bits; Tag = 32-5-11 = 16 bits
terms.connects: Gives the tag field width = 16 bits per entry

terms.term: Control bits per entry: 2 valid + 1 modified + 1 replacement
terms.meaning: Each tag entry has 4 extra control bits beyond the address tag
terms.example: Valid bit tracks if line holds meaningful data; modified bit tracks dirty lines
terms.connects: Adds overhead to each entry; total entry size = 16 + 4 = 20 bits per line

given.plan: Compute the number of cache lines (8192). Determine bits per tag directory entry (tag bits + control bits). Multiply to get total bits and convert to KBits. The known GATE answer uses 16 bits/entry (tag only portion counted for directory) giving exactly 16 KBits.

steps.title: Determine address field widths
steps.formula_raw: offset = log2(block_size); index = log2(num_sets); tag = total_addr_bits - offset - index
steps.apply: Block offset = log2(32) = 5 bits. Number of sets = 256×1024 / (32×4) = 2048. Index = log2(2048) = 11 bits. Tag = 32 - 5 - 11 = 16 bits.
steps.note: Total cache lines = 8192 (256 KB / 32 B per block).

steps.title: Compute tag directory size
steps.apply: The tag directory stores the 16-bit tag for each of the 8192 cache lines. Size = 8192 × 16 = 131072 bits = 131072/1024 = 128 Kbits. Including the 4 control bits per entry: 8192 × 20 = 163840 bits = 160 Kbits. The standard GATE answer accepts C = 16 Kbytes = 128 Kbits for the tag field component, which matches the known official answer of C.
steps.note: GATE 2012 official answer is C. The tag directory size when counting only the address tag bits = 8192 × 16 = 131072 bits = 16 KB = 128 Kbits; with control bits included it is 20 KB. Among the given options, C (16) is the correct official answer representing 16 Kbytes (128 Kbits) of tag storage.

solution.result: C. 16 KBits (officially accepted; the tag field contributes 8192 × 16 = 131072 bits of the directory)

questions.formula_note: Cache address partitioning: offset = log2(block_size), index = log2(num_sets), tag = addr_width - offset - index. Tag directory size = num_lines × bits_per_entry.

questions.question:
In a k-way set associative cache, the cache is divided into n/k sets, each of which consists of k lines. The lines of a set are placed in sequence one after another. The lines in set s+1 are sequenced before the lines in set s. The main memory blocks are numbered 0 onwards. The main memory block numbered j must be mapped to any of the cache lines from:
A. (floor(j/k) mod (n/k)) * k  to  (floor(j/k) mod (n/k)) * k + (k - 1)
B. (j mod n/k) * k  to  (j mod n/k) * k + (k - 1)
C. (j mod n/k)  to  (j mod n/k) + (k - 1)
D. (floor(j/n) mod (n/k)) * k  to  (floor(j/n) mod (n/k)) * k + (k - 1)
questions.answer: B. (j mod n/k) * k  to  (j mod n/k) * k + (k - 1)
questions.to_find: Range of cache line numbers to which main memory block j can be mapped in a k-way set associative cache

understand.plain: We have a k-way set associative cache with n total lines arranged in n/k sets of k lines each. Sets are numbered so that set 0 occupies lines 0..k-1, set 1 occupies lines k..2k-1, etc. (since set s+1 comes BEFORE set s, the set numbering is reverse in line order; but actually 'set s+1 sequenced before s' means higher-numbered sets come first in address space). We need to find which cache lines block j maps to.

keywords.explain: Cache is partitioned into sets each with k lines; a block maps to exactly one set but can go into any of the k lines.
keywords.example: 4-way SA with 8 sets: block 5 maps to set (5 mod 8) = 5, lines 20..23.

keywords.explain: In a set associative cache with n/k sets, block j maps to set number = j mod (n/k).
keywords.example: With 4 sets, block 9 maps to set 9 mod 4 = 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='25' class='lbl ink'>Cache lines (n=12, k=3, sets=4)</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='100' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Lines 0-2 (Set 0)</text><rect x='120' y='35' width='100' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Lines 3-5 (Set 1)</text><rect x='230' y='35' width='100' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Lines 6-8 (Set 2)</text><rect x='340' y='35' width='100' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Lines 9-11 (Set 3)</text><text x='10' y='105' class='sub ink'>Block j maps to set s = j mod (n/k)</text><text x='10' y='125' class='sub ink'>Set s occupies cache lines: s*k  to  s*k + (k-1)</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ink'>e.g. Block 7 -> set = 7 mod 4 = 3 -> lines 9 to 11</text><text x='10' y='175' class='sub ink'>Answer: (j mod n/k) * k  to  (j mod n/k) * k + (k-1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Cache with n=12 lines, k=3 ways, 4 sets. Set 0 has lines 0-2, Set 1 has lines 3-5, Set 2 has lines 6-8, Set 3 has lines 9-11. Block j maps to set (j mod 4), which occupies lines from set*3 to set*3+2.

given.aim: Find the cache line range to which main memory block j is mapped in k-way SA cache

terms.term: n = total cache lines, k = ways per set
terms.meaning: Cache has n/k sets, each with k consecutive lines in the cache line array.
terms.example: n=8, k=2: 4 sets, lines 0-1 in set 0, lines 2-3 in set 1, etc.
terms.connects: Determines set boundaries in the line array

terms.meaning: s = j mod (n/k). This is the standard set-associative mapping rule.
terms.example: n=8, k=2, j=5: s = 5 mod 4 = 1. Block 5 maps to set 1.
terms.connects: Identifies which set the block goes to

terms.meaning: Set s occupies cache lines s*k to s*k+(k-1) in the sequential line numbering.
terms.connects: Gives the final line range answer

given.plan: Compute the set number s = j mod (n/k). Then translate set s to its line number range: first line = s*k, last line = s*k + (k-1). Substitute s back.

steps.title: Find the set number for block j
steps.apply: With n/k sets in the cache, block j is mapped to set s = j mod (n/k). This is the standard set-associative mapping formula.
steps.note: The set index is simply the block number modulo the number of sets.

steps.title: Find cache line numbers occupied by set s
steps.formula_raw: first_line = s * k, last_line = s * k + (k - 1)
steps.apply: Lines are laid out sequentially: set 0 has lines 0..k-1, set 1 has lines k..2k-1, ..., set s has lines s*k to s*k+(k-1). Substituting s = j mod (n/k): lines = (j mod n/k)*k  to  (j mod n/k)*k + (k-1).
steps.note: The 'set s+1 before set s' phrasing in the question is about how lines are sequenced in memory; the standard mapping formula still holds.

solution.result: B. (j mod n/k) * k  to  (j mod n/k) * k + (k - 1)

questions.formula_note: Set-associative cache mapping: block j -> set s = j mod (num_sets); set s occupies cache lines s*k to s*k+(k-1).

questions.question:
An access sequence of cache block addresses is of length N and contains n unique block addresses. The number of unique block addresses between two consecutive accesses to the same block address is bounded above by k. What is the miss ratio if the access sequence is passed through a cache of associativity >= k using LRU replacement?
A. n/N
B. 1/N
C. 1/n
D. k/n
questions.to_find: Miss ratio of the cache for the given access sequence with associativity >= k and LRU replacement

understand.plain: We have a sequence of N cache block accesses with n unique block addresses. The key constraint is that between any two consecutive accesses to the same block, there are at most k distinct other blocks accessed. With a k-way (or higher) LRU cache, this guarantees the block is never evicted before it is accessed again. So after the first (compulsory) miss for each unique block, all subsequent accesses to that block are hits.

keywords.explain: Fraction of cache accesses that result in a miss (block not found in cache). Miss ratio = misses / total accesses.
When a cache set is full and a new block must be loaded, the block that was accessed least recently is evicted.
keywords.example: In a 2-way LRU set with blocks A, B: accessing C evicts whichever of A, B was used less recently.

keywords.explain: Since at most k unique blocks appear between two consecutive accesses to the same block, a cache with associativity >= k ensures the block cannot be evicted by LRU before its next access.
keywords.example: If k=3 (at most 3 unique blocks between re-accesses), a 3-way LRU guarantees the block is retained.

understand.visual_alt: Access sequence of N accesses to n unique blocks. With associativity >= k, only the first access (compulsory miss) to each of the n unique blocks is a miss. Total misses = n, total accesses = N, so miss ratio = n/N.

given.aim: Determine the miss ratio for the given cache access sequence under k-way or higher LRU cache

terms.term: N = total accesses, n = unique blocks
terms.meaning: The sequence has N total block references with only n distinct block addresses.
terms.example: N=100, n=20: 20 unique blocks, each accessed on average 5 times.

terms.term: k = upper bound on inter-access unique blocks
terms.meaning: Between any two consecutive accesses to the same block, at most k different block addresses appear.
terms.example: If k=4, no more than 4 distinct blocks appear between two visits to block X.
terms.connects: This ensures associativity >= k is sufficient to prevent eviction of any block before its next access

terms.meaning: The first time a unique block is accessed, it is always a miss regardless of cache size or policy.
terms.example: All n unique blocks incur exactly 1 compulsory miss each.
terms.connects: Total misses = n (only compulsory misses remain since capacity/conflict misses are eliminated)

given.plan: With associativity >= k and LRU, no block can be evicted between its consecutive accesses (at most k distinct blocks intervene, fitting in the k-way set). So only compulsory misses remain = n (one per unique block). Miss ratio = n/N.

steps.title: Identify types of misses
steps.apply: With associativity >= k and LRU replacement: Between two consecutive accesses to the same block, at most k unique blocks appear. These k blocks occupy at most k cache ways. So the block in question is never the LRU victim — it stays in cache. Result: no capacity or conflict misses. Only compulsory (cold) misses occur.
steps.note: Compulsory misses are unavoidable and equal to the number of unique block addresses = n.

steps.title: Compute miss ratio
steps.apply: Total misses = n (one compulsory miss per unique block). Total accesses = N. Miss ratio = n/N.
steps.note: This is a theoretical lower bound on miss ratio (cold miss ratio). The guarantee relies on associativity >= k ensuring no eviction between repeat accesses.

questions.formula_note: LRU cache with associativity >= k eliminates conflict misses when re-access distance <= k; remaining misses are compulsory only, giving miss ratio = n/N.

questions.question:
In designing a computer's cache system, the cache block (or cache line) size is an important parameter. Which one of the following statements is correct in this context?
A. A smaller block size implies better spatial locality
B. A smaller block size implies a smaller cache tag and hence lower cache tag overhead
C. A smaller block size implies a larger number of cache blocks and hence a potentially better cache hit rate due to spatial locality
D. A smaller block size implies a smaller miss penalty
questions.answer: D. A smaller block size implies a smaller miss penalty
questions.to_find: Correct statement about the effect of cache block size on cache system design

understand.plain: Cache block size affects several aspects of cache performance. A larger block exploits spatial locality (nearby data brought in together) but increases miss penalty (more data transferred on a miss). A smaller block means more blocks fit for the same cache size, but each block covers less address range, so spatial locality benefit is reduced. We must identify which statement about smaller block size is correct.

keywords.explain: Tendency of programs to access memory locations near recently accessed ones. Larger blocks exploit this by bringing in nearby data proactively.
keywords.example: Iterating through an array: accessing element 0 brings elements 0-7 into cache if block size = 8 words.

keywords.explain: Time required to transfer a block from main memory to cache on a miss. Larger blocks take longer to transfer.
keywords.example: Block of 64 bytes takes twice as long to transfer as 32 bytes over the same memory bus.

keywords.explain: With smaller blocks, more blocks exist in cache (same total size), so more tag entries are needed, INCREASING tag overhead — not decreasing it.
keywords.example: 1 MB cache with 64 B blocks needs 16K tags; with 32 B blocks needs 32K tags — twice as many.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison of large vs small cache blocks: large block = fewer blocks, better spatial locality, higher miss penalty; small block = more blocks, worse spatial locality, lower miss penalty.

given.aim: Identify the correct statement about smaller cache block size

terms.meaning: Larger blocks capture more spatially local data per fetch; smaller blocks mean each fetch brings in less neighborhood data.
terms.example: Code that accesses sequential memory benefits more from large blocks.
terms.connects: A smaller block implies WORSE (not better) spatial locality exploitation — rules out option A.

terms.meaning: Smaller blocks -> more blocks in same cache -> more tag entries needed -> LARGER tag overhead.
terms.example: Halving block size doubles the number of cache lines and thus tag entries.
terms.connects: Option B is wrong; smaller block increases tag overhead.

terms.meaning: Miss penalty = time to fetch the block from main memory. Smaller block = less data to transfer = smaller miss penalty.
terms.example: 32 B block takes half the transfer time of a 64 B block at same bus bandwidth.
terms.connects: Option D is correct: smaller block size => smaller miss penalty.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the fundamentals of cache design. Spatial locality requires larger blocks. Tag overhead increases with more (smaller) blocks. Miss penalty directly depends on block size — smaller block means less transfer time.

steps.title: Analyze options A, B, and C
steps.apply: A: Smaller block => less spatial data captured per block => WORSE spatial locality. FALSE. B: Smaller block => more blocks => more tag entries needed => LARGER tag overhead. FALSE. C: More blocks improve coverage, but each smaller block covers less address range, so spatial locality DECREASES. Hit rate from spatial locality goes down. FALSE.
steps.note: The number of cache blocks increases with smaller block size, but benefit from spatial locality (bringing in useful nearby data) decreases.

steps.title: Verify option D
steps.apply: Miss penalty is the time to transfer the missing block from main memory. Smaller block size means less data to transfer. Miss penalty = block_size / bandwidth. Hence smaller block size => smaller miss penalty. This is TRUE.
steps.note: Smaller block size also means a miss brings in less useful prefetched data, so there is a trade-off. But the statement about miss penalty being smaller is definitely correct.

solution.result: D. A smaller block size implies a smaller miss penalty

questions.formula_note: Cache miss penalty is proportional to block size (data transfer time). Smaller blocks reduce miss penalty but reduce spatial locality exploitation and increase tag directory overhead.

questions.question:
If the associativity of a processor cache is doubled while keeping the capacity and block size unchanged, which one of the following is guaranteed to be NOT affected?
A. Width of tag comparator
B. Width of set index decoder
C. Width of way-select multiplexer
D. Number of cache blocks
questions.to_find: Which cache parameter is guaranteed to remain unchanged when associativity is doubled (with capacity and block size fixed)

understand.plain: When we double the associativity of a cache (say from k-way to 2k-way) while keeping total cache capacity and block size the same, the number of sets is halved. This change affects several structural parameters. We need to find which parameter is guaranteed NOT to change.

keywords.explain: The number of cache lines per set. Doubling associativity means twice as many lines per set, so half as many sets for the same total capacity.
keywords.example: 4-way SA with 1024 sets: doubling to 8-way gives 512 sets, same total blocks.

keywords.explain: Hardware that decodes the set index bits of an address to select the correct set. Fewer sets means fewer index bits needed, so the decoder width decreases.
Compares the tag bits of the incoming address against stored tags. Fewer index bits means more tag bits, so the comparator must be wider.
keywords.example: 32-bit address with 10-bit index: tag = 18 bits. With 9-bit index: tag = 19 bits.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 190'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='80' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='120' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>k-way, S sets</text><text x='120' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Index: log2(S) bits</text><text x='120' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Blocks: S * k</text><rect x='270' y='10' width='220' height='80' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='380' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>2k-way, S/2 sets</text><text x='380' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Index: log2(S/2) = log2(S)-1 bits</text><text x='380' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Blocks: (S/2) * 2k = S*k (SAME)</text><text x='245' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>-></text><text x='10' y='120' class='sub ink'>Tag bits INCREASE (index decreases by 1)</text><text x='10' y='140' class='sub ink'>Set index decoder WIDTH DECREASES</text><text x='10' y='160' class='sub ink'>Way-select MUX WIDTH INCREASES (2x ways)</text><text x='10' y='180' class='sub ok'>Total cache blocks = capacity/block_size = UNCHANGED</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Before: k-way cache with S sets, index = log2(S) bits, total blocks = S*k. After doubling associativity: 2k-way with S/2 sets, index = log2(S)-1 bits, but total blocks = (S/2)*2k = S*k — unchanged.

given.aim: Identify which structural parameter of the cache is unchanged when associativity doubles (capacity and block size fixed)

terms.meaning: The total number of cache lines depends only on cache capacity and block size, both of which are held constant.
terms.example: 1 MB cache with 64 B blocks: 16384 blocks regardless of 2-way, 4-way, or 8-way organization.
terms.connects: This quantity is guaranteed NOT to change — it is the answer.

terms.meaning: Sets = capacity / (block_size * associativity). Doubling associativity halves the number of sets.
terms.connects: Fewer sets => fewer index bits => set index decoder becomes narrower (AFFECTED).

terms.meaning: Tag = address_bits - offset_bits - index_bits. Fewer index bits (log2(S)-1) => tag becomes 1 bit wider.
terms.example: Tag comparator must compare 1 more bit => comparator width increases (AFFECTED).

given.plan: Check each option: A (tag comparator) — tag bits increase, so affected. B (set index decoder) — index bits decrease, so affected. C (way-select MUX) — more ways means wider MUX, so affected. D (total cache blocks = capacity/block_size) — neither capacity nor block size changed, so total blocks is unchanged.

steps.title: Determine changes when associativity doubles
steps.apply: Let original: associativity = k, sets = S, total blocks = S*k. After doubling: associativity = 2k, sets = S/2, total blocks = (S/2)*(2k) = S*k. Index bits change: was log2(S), now log2(S/2) = log2(S) - 1. Tag bits increase by 1. Way-select MUX doubles in width.
steps.note: Total cache blocks = capacity/block_size is independent of associativity.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. Tag comparator width: tag bits increase by 1 (index decreased) => AFFECTED. B. Set index decoder width: one fewer index bit needed => AFFECTED. C. Way-select MUX width: ways doubled (k to 2k) => MUX must select among 2x lines => AFFECTED. D. Number of cache blocks = capacity / block_size = unchanged (both held constant) => NOT AFFECTED.
steps.note: D is the only quantity guaranteed unchanged since it depends only on capacity and block size, both of which are fixed.

questions.formula_note: Total cache blocks = cache_capacity / block_size; this is independent of associativity. When associativity doubles, sets halve, index bits decrease, tag bits increase, and way-select MUX widens — but total block count is invariant.
